coredata.dc:title: Sonochemical synthesis of iron oxide nanoparticles loaded with folate and cisplatin: Effect of ultrasonic frequency
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Dolores, Reyman
Raquel, Serrano
Adianez, Garcia-Leis
coredata.dc:description: Simple preparative methods were used to sonosynthesize different magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (FeNPs) via co-precipitation of aqueous solutions of ferrous salts in a basic aqueous solution of ethylene glycol (EG). Sonosynthesis was achieved using different frequencies of ultrasound: 581, 861, and 1141kHz under the same acoustic power. The hydroxyl radicals generated by cavitational collapse, induced by the ultrasonic field, led to the oxidation of Fe2+ to Fe3+. The rate of sonochemical Fe3+ production decreased linearly with the frequency. Three different systems of FeNPs were synthesized, all with the same core but a different shell: FeNPs capped with EG (EG/FeNPs), FeNPs capped with EG and folate (Fol/EG/FeNPs), and FeNPs capped with EG, folate and cisplatin (Pt/EG/FeNPs). The nanoparticles were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, fluorescence and Raman microspectroscopy, total-reflection X-ray fluorescence, and elemental analysis (C, N, and H). The magnetization hysteresis loops of these samples were also measured. The obtained values of saturation magnetization were within the interval between 60 and 93Am2 kg−1. From the analysis of these results, it was found that the ultrasonic frequency did not affect the nanoparticle size (diameter of 21–31nm). In contrast, the frequency affected the amount of drug loaded, as cisplatin loading increased proportionately with ultrasound frequency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetic nanoparticle
Iron oxide
Folate
Cisplatin
Sonochemistry
Ultrasonic frequency effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of ultrasonic frequency on the oxidation of Fe2+ to Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectra of ferrous ammonium sulfate (1mM) and sodium chloride (1mM) dissolved in sulfuric acid (0.5M) at different times of sonication (0–30min).
Effect of ultrasonic frequency on the Fe3+ production (observed by the increment of absorbance at 304nm) at different sonication times.
Linear relationship between the ultrasonic frequency and the relative rate of Fe3+ production and Pt absorption.
Raman spectra of EG/FeNP samples sonosynthesized using different ultrasonic frequency (581, 861 and 1140kHz, 15min of reaction time).
Raman spectra of FeNP samples sonosynthesized at 1180kHz (15min of reaction time) and coated with different compounds.
Raman spectra of EG/FeNP581 sample dipped 30 and 60min in an aqueous solution of folate (1mM).
Raman spectrum of the Pt/Fol/EG/FeNP581 sample sonosynthesized with a reaction time of 35min.
TEM image of Fol/EG/FeNPs sonosynthesized at 861kHz.

(A) Confocal microscope image of Fol/EG/FeNP861. (B) Confocal fluorescence image of A (exciting at 364nm). Fluorescence spectra of folate in aqueous solution (red) and recorded in the point marked by the arrow (green). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Magnetization curve of EG/FeNP861 sample at 250K. The inset is the hysteresis loop on an enlarged scale.
ZFC and FC magnetization curves of EG/FeNP861 sample under an applied field of 100 Oe.
Percentage of carbon, hydrogen and nitrogen in the FeNP samples.
Number of atoms of the element relative to 100 iron atoms in the samples containing cisplatin.
Saturation magnetization, remanence magnetization and coercivity values obtained at 298K.

head.ce:title: Sonochemical synthesis of iron oxide nanoparticles loaded with folate and cisplatin: Effect of ultrasonic frequency

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química Física Aplicada, Universidad Autónoma de Madrid, 28049 Madrid, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Instituto de Estructura de la Materia, IEM-CSIC, Serrano 121, 28006 Madrid, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Instituto de Estructura de la Materia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Simple preparative methods were used to sonosynthesize different magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (FeNPs) via co-precipitation of aqueous solutions of ferrous salts in a basic aqueous solution of ethylene glycol (EG). Sonosynthesis was achieved using different frequencies of ultrasound: 581, 861, and 1141kHz under the same acoustic power. The hydroxyl radicals generated by cavitational collapse, induced by the ultrasonic field, led to the oxidation of Fe2+ to Fe3+. The rate of sonochemical Fe3+ production decreased linearly with the frequency. Three different systems of FeNPs were synthesized, all with the same core but a different shell: FeNPs capped with EG (EG/FeNPs), FeNPs capped with EG and folate (Fol/EG/FeNPs), and FeNPs capped with EG, folate and cisplatin (Pt/EG/FeNPs). The nanoparticles were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, fluorescence and Raman microspectroscopy, total-reflection X-ray fluorescence, and elemental analysis (C, N, and H). The magnetization hysteresis loops of these samples were also measured. The obtained values of saturation magnetization were within the interval between 60 and 93Am2 kg−1. From the analysis of these results, it was found that the ultrasonic frequency did not affect the nanoparticle size (diameter of 21–31nm). In contrast, the frequency affected the amount of drug loaded, as cisplatin loading increased proportionately with ultrasound frequency.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron oxide nanoparticles (FeNPs) have been the subject of great interest and intensive study for biomedical applications, for example as contrast agents for magnetic resonance imaging [1], hyperthermia agents for anti-cancer strategies [2,3], and magnetic targeted carriers of drugs [4,5]. Our main interest is in the use of FeNPs as magnetic drug delivery systems. The magnetic fluids used in this application are based on magnetite or maghemite nanoparticles dispersed in water. A small size (<100nm), uniform morphology, and low dispersity are required for the practical use of FeNPs in biotechnology.

Many methods have been developed to prepare superparamagnetic FeNPs. The FeNPs prepared by thermal decomposition [6,7] in organic solvents are highly hydrophobic, which hampers their biomedical application. A common method to synthesize hydrophilic FeNPs involves the co-precipitation of aqueous solutions of ferric and ferrous salts in a basic aqueous solution [8,9]. This method is simple and it is easy to obtain high yields; however, this co-precipitation process does not provide good control over the size distribution of FeNPs. Sonochemistry has proven to be an important tool in the preparation of metal oxides. Different sonochemical synthesis of FeNPs have been published [10–13]. Some of these studies have demonstrated that the direct decomposition of iron precursors Fe(CO)5 (using a high-intensity ultrasonic horn, 20kHz), in the presence of different surfactants, produces stable colloids of coated Fe2O3 nanoparticles 5–16nm in diameter [14]. Using a similar ultrasonic device, the synthesis of FeNPs by sonochemical oxidation of an aqueous solution of iron(II) acetate was achieved under 1.5atm of Ar at 298K for 3h [15,16]. In this case, the size of the FeNPs synthesized, around 10nm, was small enough to exhibit superparamagnetic behavior. Also, hollow hematite FeNPs were prepared by irradiation of a mixture of carbon nanoparticles and iron pentacarbonyl in hexadecane, using an ultrasound frequency of 20kHz [17]. The sonochemical reaction of Fe(OH)2 in diethylene or triethylene glycol/water solutions allows for the easy preparation of FeNPs with a size of around 10–30nm [18]. Due to the presence of glycol groups on the particle surface, carboxylic groups can covalently anchor to these systems [19].

Another goal of this work was to load these systems with an anticancer agent. Cisplatin is a chemotherapeutic drug that is highly effective in the treatment of solid tumors. Nevertheless, its use is restricted because small doses produce considerable side effects and there is rapid development of drug resistance. Nanoparticle-based drug delivery vehicles have the potential to overcome some of these limitations because they allow selective delivery of the drug to the tumor, which increases the therapeutic response and lowers toxicity. The attachment of cisplatin to FeNPs has already been accomplished. However, this drug rapidly dissociates from FeNPs due to inefficient binding [20–22]. This can result in the early release of cisplatin while still in the bloodstream and the failure of the drug to reach the tumor. Therefore, there is a need to develop more stable iron oxide nanoparticles that can retain platinum drugs more strongly on their surfaces. Recently, Wagstaff et al. synthesized [23] a system containing a gold coating over an iron oxide core to which the active component of cisplatin was attached using a thiolated polyethylene glycol linker. Pt/Au/FeNPs were obtained with diameters of 60–120nm. Toxicity studies demonstrated that these nanoparticles were up to 110-fold more cytotoxic than cisplatin.

However, we were not satisfied with only loading the vehicle with drugs, as we also wanted to build a Trojan horse. Most of the available anticancer agents cannot distinguish between cancerous and healthy cells, leading to systematic toxicity and undesired side effects. A major challenge to successful chemotherapy is achieving specific drug accumulation at tumor sites. Folate receptors are selectively overexpressed on the cancer cell surface, so nanocarriers (FeNPs) conjugated with folate may help to achieve specific accumulation in cancer cells. Our goal was to coat FeNPs with ethylene glycol (EG) and folate.

On the other hand, knowledge of the chemical composition of the FeNP core is essential for the evaluation of these magnetic nanoparticles as targeted carriers of drugs. Depending on the details of the preparation method, different iron oxides can be present in the samples. Three of the most common are hematite (α-Fe2O3), maghemite (γ-Fe2O3), and magnetite (Fe3O4) [24]. Hematite is the most common iron oxide in nature because of its high thermodynamic stability, and maghemite is an important intermediate in hematite formation from the oxidation of magnetite precursors. Different studies [25–28] have demonstrated that Raman spectroscopy is a good tool for rapidly distinguishing iron oxides, hydroxides, and (oxy)hydroxides and, in our view, is a more powerful technique than X-ray diffraction. The Raman spectrum of hematite presents narrow bands at 225 (very strong), 247 (very weak), 292 (very strong), 411 (moderate), 496 (very weak), 610 (weak)cm−1. The Raman spectrum of magnetite presents an intense band at 668cm−1 and much smaller bands at 193, 306, and 538cm−1 [29]. With regard to maghemite, the majority of authors take three broad maxima around 370, 500, and 710cm−1 as being indicative of maghemite.

We report here the development of multifunctional nanocarriers with a magnetic core and a biocompatible shell of ethylene glycol (EG), with small amounts of folate and the anticancer agent cisplatin on the surface. These nanocarriers were synthesized in different ultrasonic fields (580, 861, and 1140kHz) and were characterized by different techniques.

Ferrous ammonium sulfate, ferrous sulfate ·7 hydrate and NaOH were purchased by Panreac, cisplatin and folic acid were purchased by Sigma–Aldrich and EG was purchased by Merck (analysis grade). Deionized water was used for the preparation of all aqueous solutions.

The ultrasound equipment used in these experiments was a Meinhardt Ultraschalltechnick high frequency sonicator with one transducer operating at 581, 861 and 1141kHz. In all cases, the experiments were conducted at constant acoustic power. The acoustic power measurements were carried out using a calorimetric method. Based on the assumption that the mechanical energy generated by the ultrasonic waves is reduced to heat, the dissipated ultrasonic power, Pc
, was calculated from the rate of temperature increase as:
P
c
( W ) =
C
p
M
d T
d t
where Cp is the heat capacity of water at constant pressure (4182Jkg−1 K−1), M is the mass of water (kg) and dT/dt is temperature rise per second. For three frequencies, Pc value was 29W.

The normal Raman spectra were obtained on a Renishaw Raman Microscope System RM2000 equipped with a charge-coupled device (CCD) camera, using a He/Ne 632.8nm laser as excitation source. The laser beam was focused on the solid sample through the x50 objective that also collected the scattered radiation. The laser spot on the sample was 1.5 um. The spectra resolution was 1cm−1. All spectra were acquired at room temperature (20°C). The laser power was adjusted to guarantee no sample decomposition. In Raman spectroscopy, the excessive exposure of iron oxide sample to laser radiation has been shown to generate hematite. This oxidation process is principally due to heating and not to the photo-effect [29].

For total-reflection X-ray fluorescence (TXRF) measurements, an EXTRA-II Rich & Seifert X-ray fluorescence spectrometer, equipped with Mo radiation was employed. Excitation conditions were 50kV and 602μA, and the acquisition time for each spectrum was 500s.
The analysis of C, H and N of the samples was realized using an Elemental Analyzer LECO CHNS-932 and MX5 Mettler Toledo microbalance with 0.1μg of resolution.

Particle size and shape were analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) in a JEOL-2000 FXII electron microscope operated at 200keV. The samples were prepared by placing one drops of the colloidal solution onto a carbon-coated copper grid (200 mesh) and drying it. The mean particle size and distribution were calculated by measuring the internal dimension of at least 100 particles.

Fluorescent images were acquired on two confocal laser scanning microscopes: Leica TCS SP2 for ultraviolet lines at 351 and 364nm and TCS SP5 for visible line at 405nm. For these observations, in the first place, aqueous colloidal solution of the samples were prepared in a basic medium (pH=12) and filtered with a polyester filter of 0.45μm pore size. One or two drop of colloid were deposited on a slide and dried in closed environment.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu spectrofluorimeter RF-5301 PC with a 3nm bandwidth in excitation and a 1.5nm bandwidth in emission. Absorption spectra were obtained using a Hewlett–Packard UV–vis spectrophotometer HP 8453.

Magnetic measurements were carried out at room temperature using a MPMS superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) magnetometer. Magnetization hysteresis loops were measured up to fields of 3T. Magnetic relaxation measurements were performed after cooling in a field of 100 Oe from 10K to 298K increasing 2K/min.

FeNPs were synthesized via a co-precipitation process of iron(II) sulfate (in the absence or presence of cisplatin) over an aqueous solution of sodium hydroxide and EG, in the absence or presence of folate.

Three different types of experiments were performed:
(1)
50mL of iron(II) sulfate (0.4M) added dropwise to 100mL of an aqueous solution of NaOH (0.2M) and EG (0.18M). Samples: EG/FeNPs581 (synthesized using a 581kHz frequency), EG/FeNPs861 (861kHz), and EG/FeNPs1141 (1141kHz).
(2)
50mL of iron(II) sulfate (0.4M) added dropwise to 100mL of an aqueous solution of NaOH (0.2M), EG (0.18M), and folic acid (1.13mM). Samples: Fol/EG/NPs581, Fol/EG/NPs861, and Fol/EG/NPs1141.
(3)
50mL of iron(II) sulfate (0.4M) and cisplatin (0.5mM) added dropwise to 100mL of an aqueous solution of NaOH (0.2M), EG (0.18M), and folic acid (1.13mM). Samples: Pt/Fol/EG/NPs581, Pt/Fol/EG/NPs861, and Pt/Fol/EG/NPs1141.

In all reactions, the drip rate was 4mL/min and reaction time was 15min. Each experiment was performed using a different ultrasonic frequency (581, 861, and 1140kHz). The temperature interval during the reaction was 317–321K. In all cases, a black precipitate was isolated and washed with deionized water up to pH 10. The precipitate was centrifuged twice with water and once with ethanol. As the final step, the precipitate was dried in an oven at 60°C for 12h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of ultrasonic frequency on the oxidation of Fe2+ to Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to study the effect of ultrasonic frequency on Fe3+ production, ferrous ammonium sulfate (1mM) and sodium chloride (1mM) dissolved in sulfuric acid (0.5M) were irradiated for different periods of time using three different frequencies: 581, 861 and 1141kHz. In Fig. 1 , the variations in the absorbance spectra of this solution with sonication time (using 581kHz) are represented. The new maximum (around 304nm) that appears in these spectra is associated with the oxidation of Fe2+ to Fe3+, which is mediated by the hydroxyl radicals generated by cavitational collapse induced by ultrasound [30]. Similar findings were obtained at 861 and 1141kHz. The effect of the ultrasound frequency on Fe3+ production is represented in Fig. 2 . A linear dependence of Fe3+ generation on the sonication time, together with a decrease in the yield of Fe3+ with an increase in frequency can be observed. From the slopes of these straight lines, it is possible to obtain the relative oxidation rates. Surprisingly, these rates varied linearly with the frequency of sonication (Fig. 3 ). Up to now, such a clear relationship between the reaction rate and frequency has not been observed. Partial explanations for the influence of frequency on the oxidation process can be found in the literature. Most studies were designed to establish differences in reaction rates between irradiation at higher frequencies (⩾100kHz) and irradiation at lower frequencies (20–40kHz). The first study on this topic was published in 1992 by Petrier et al. [31]. In this important paper, the effectiveness of irradiation at 20 and 514kHz was compared by investigating the oxidation of aqueous KI to iodine and the generation of hydrogen peroxide in water at the same input power. Irradiation at higher frequencies (514kHz) led to greater enhancements in the rates of both reactions. These authors related these enhancements to the resonance radius and the duration of the bubble collapse, since these parameters are lower at higher frequencies. These conditions favor more efficient ejection of
OH radicals from bubbles before they have time to recombine inside the cavitation bubble.

Similar works can be found in the literature in frequency interval higher than 100kHz [32–35]. In these studies, the frequency effect on oxidation process is the same as that observed by us. So, Kanthale and co-workers [32] found that both H2O2 production and sonoluminescence intensity decrease with increasing frequency in the range 213–1056kHz. The same behavior was found by Pétrier and co-workers when they examined the sonochemical degradation of phenol [33], chlorophenols [34] and bisphenol [35] in the range of 200–800kHz. The observed dependence of frequency on chemical process can be attributed to several factors; for example, when the frequency increases, the cavitation threshold becomes higher, the maximum temperature attained in the collapse decreases, the lifetime of bubbles becomes shorter and the size of cavitating bubbles decreases. On the other hand, recent studies performed using numerical simulations showed that the rate of energy accumulation also decreased sharply with increasing frequency in the range of 200–1000kHz [36]. In this case, the bubbles did not have enough time to store energy because the duration of collapse was shorter at higher frequencies. All these factors lead to relate this behavior with a lower yield of
OH generation as the frequency is increased.

Depending on the reaction conditions, the FeNP core can be formed of different iron oxides. Thus, knowledge of the chemical composition is essential, because not all the oxides have the same magnetic properties. Raman spectroscopy has proved an important tool for the analysis of iron oxides because magnetite, maghemite and hematite present different bands between them (very different in the case of hematite). So, an intense maximum at 670cm−1 is associated with magnetite (Fe3O4) [25] and an intense maximum at 705cm−1with maghemite (γ-Fe2O3). Other less intense bands around 368 and 492cm−1 do not serve to distinguish between these two oxides because these appear in both magnetite and maghemite. Fig. 4 shows the Raman spectra of samples synthesized at different ultrasonic frequencies with a reaction time of 15min. Similar spectra were observed at 581, 861, and 1141kHz with small differences. In this manner, the three samples analyzed in Fig. 4 show a maximum at 670cm−1 and, overlapping with this, a peak at 705cm−1. In the sample obtained at 861kHz, the peak at 705cm−1 is less intense than in the other samples; indicating that its core is composed mainly of magnetite. The magnetization assessments presented in the last section confirm these results (magnetite is more magnetic than maghemite). Therefore, from these spectra, we can conclude that, after 15min of reaction, for the three frequencies, the core of sonosynthesized FeNPs was composed of a mixture of magnetite and maghemite, thereby guaranteeing the magnetic properties of the sample.

The Raman spectra of all samples changed when the sonication time was 35min. Fig. 7 shows the Raman spectra of the Pt/Fol/EG/FeNP581 sample sonosynthesized with this reaction time. The group of narrow bands in the low frequency region between 100 and 800cm−1 (225 (strong), 241 (very weak), 290 (very strong), 413 (medium), 494 (very weak), and 611 (weak) in cm−1) indicates the presence of hematite (α-Fe2O3). Thus, a longer period of sonication induced total oxidation of the sample.

Fig. 8 shows a TEM image of Fol/EG/FeNPs obtained at 861kHz. This figure shows the near-spherical shape of these nanoparticles. Similar images were obtained for the other samples. The mean sizes measured for the FeNPs were: 21–23nm for samples with EG as the surfactant, 24–26nm for samples with EG/folate and 29–31nm for the samples with EG/folate/cisplatin. These results show that the surfactant slightly modifies the size of the nanoparticle and the frequency does not affect the size. Initially, we expected a decrease in FeNP size with ultrasound frequency because the generation of Fe3+ is reduced at higher frequencies. However, these results demonstrate that Fe3+ generation is not the rate-determining step in the formation of FeNPs. Therefore, for the three frequencies, the amount of Fe3+ (generated by ultrasound) was high enough so that it did not affect the synthesis of magnetite.

So far, we have demonstrated that this system consists of spherical nanoparticles with a magnetite/maghemite core; nevertheless, the presence of folate in these samples has not yet proved. The folate anion is a fluorescent compound, such that we can exploit this property to detect its presence in the samples. For this, the images of the samples were obtained using laser confocal scanning microscopy. Different wavelengths of excitation were used (351, 364, and 405nm) and an extensive green fluorescence was always observed (Fig. 9 ). In a fluorescent point of the image, the fluorescence spectrum shape was also recorded in the 420–620nm interval. A maximum around 480nm was observed, which coincided with that obtained for an aqueous folate solution in basic medium (pH=10). No green fluorescence could be clearly observed in the absence of folate. According to these experimental observations, clearly, the folate anion was part of the composition of the FeNPs.

The presence of folate was also supported by elemental analysis of C, H, and N performed on the EG/FeNP and Fol/EG/FeNP samples (Table 1 ). The C and H detected in the samples without folate confirmed the shell of EG. The nitrogen content of EG/FeNP was practically zero, which was not the case when the samples were synthesized in the presence of folate. On the other hand, we observed that the amount of folate absorbed was lower for the samples synthesized at 1141kHz. This analysis, together with the fluorescence of the samples, confirmed the presence of folate in the FeNPs synthesized in the presence of this anion. An interaction through hydrogen bonding between the amine, carboxylate and hydroxyl groups of folate anion and the hydroxyl groups of ethylene glycol can be established to explain the adsorption de this anion on the EG platform.

With the aim of evaluating the effectiveness of Pt incorporation into the FeNP, the samples synthesized in the presence of cisplatin were analyzed by TXRF. The results of qualitative inspection of the samples are shown in Table 2 . The spectra showed the presence of Fe K-lines as the main component of the FeNP, and Pt L-lines as the second most abundant component. Also, trace contamination with Si, Ti and Mn (poorer intensity) was detected, which was probably associated with contamination by the reagents used in the synthesis process. As shown in Fig. 3, the Pt/Fe ratio increased linearly with the frequency of ultrasound used in the sonosynthesis and is inversely related with the rate of Fe2+oxidation by
OH. We associated this behavior with the lower production of Fe3+ to higher frequency, which helped increase the absorption of cisplatin.

Hysteresis loops (Fig. 10 ) were obtained at 300K for the nine samples. The magnetization curves were characteristic of maghemite NPs of more than 12nm in size, exhibiting a magnetic hysteresis loop at room temperature. These obtained hysteresis loops were symmetric around the origin. From these curves, it was possible to obtain saturation magnetization (Ms), remanence magnetization (Mr), and coercivity (Hc) values. The obtained value of Ms was determined by extrapolating the M vs. 1/H plot to infinity field. As shown in Table 3 , the Ms values were always above 60Am2 kg−1. A value of 93Am2 kg−1 was achieved for the Fol/EG/FeNP581 sample. These differences were attributed to the proportion of magnetite vs. maghemite in the core. In all curves, residual magnetization and coercivity were almost zero at room temperature. Therefore, as shown also by Mizukoshi et al. [38], the FeNPs obtained by reverse precipitation methods are magnetically soft. The temperature dependence of magnetization changes in zero-field cooling (ZFC) and field cooling (FC) procedures was also observed. The ZFC-FC curves under a 100 Oe external magnetic field are shown in Fig. 11 . At low temperature, the ZFC magnetization was much lower than the FC magnetization. The ZFC curve overlapped with the FC curve at around 250K. The results showed that the particles exhibited ferromagnetic behavior at room temperature with very low Mr and Hc values, which demonstrated the high crystallinity of the samples and the proximity of the transition temperature to the superparamagnetism.

We have developed a simple and direct route for the preparation of high performance FeNPs (nanocarriers) using ultrasound of different frequencies. These new multifunctional nanocarriers were composed of a magnetic core and a biocompatible shell (EG) with small amounts of cisplatin and folate. The sonochemical rate of oxidation of Fe2+ to Fe3+ was inversely proportional to the ultrasonic frequency, leading to an increased presence of Pt in the FeNPs. The mean size of the FeNPs was quasi-independent of frequency and the stabilizers forming the shell. The samples containing folate in the structure emitted a green fluorescence, a property that can be helpful for nanobiotechnological applications. All nanoparticles exhibited ferromagnetic characteristics at room temperature, with high Ms and low Hc and Mr values.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A.G.-L. acknowledges CSIC and FSE 2007−2013 for a JAE-CSIC predoctoral grant. This work was supported with the project MAT 2009-14741-C02-02.
